title: दुर्गा अष्टोत्तर शतनामावली

॥ इति श्री दुर्गा अष्टोत्तर शतनामावली सम्पूर्ण ॥

hindiMeaning: दुर्गा अष्टोत्तर शतनामावली देवी दुर्गा के 108 पवित्र नामों का संग्रह है। इन नामों में माता दुर्गा के विभिन्न रूपों और शक्तियों का वर्णन है - दुर्गा (दुर्गम संकटों का नाश करने वाली), चण्डिका (चण्ड-मुण्ड का वध करने वाली), महिषासुरमर्दिनी (महिषासुर का वध करने वाली), कात्यायनी (कात्यायन ऋषि की पुत्री), कालरात्रि (काल का भी नाश करने वाली), जगदम्बिका (जगत की माता) आदि।
benefits: Reciting 108 names of Durga bestows complete protection of the Divine Mother
Traditionally recited for removal of all enemies, obstacles, and evil forces
Used during Navratri puja, Durga Ashtami, and Kumari Puja
Particularly powerful during Navratri and on Tuesdays and Fridays
description: Durga Ashtottara Shatanamavali (दुर्गा अष्टोत्तर शतनामावली) is a devotional recitation of 108 sacred names dedicated to Goddess Durga. Attributed to Durga Saptashati / Markandeya Purana, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Devotees recite this prayer to used during Navratri puja, Durga Ashtami, and Kumari Puja and grant courage, strength, and fearlessness. It is also said to destroy all enemies, obstacles, and evil forces. It is especially recommended on Tuesday (Mangalvar) and Friday (Shukravar) and during Navratri, Durga Ashtami, and Vijayadashami for maximum spiritual benefit. The text contains 108 verses and takes around 15 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Durga Ashtottara Shatanamavali in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Durga Ashtottara Shatanamavali - 108 names of Goddess Durga in Sanskrit with transliteration. For Navratri puja and daily archana.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Durga Ashtottara Shatanamavali?
seoFaqs.answer: The Durga Ashtottara Shatanamavali is the standard archana namavali of 108 sacred names of Goddess Durga, used during regular puja while offering kumkum, flowers and akshata to the deity. Each name is preceded by Om and followed by Namah — Om Durgayai Namah, Om Devyai Namah, Om Tripurayai Namah, and so on through 108 invocations. The names span Durga's many aspects: her identity as Adi-Shakti, her warrior forms (Mahishasura-mardini, Shumbha-Nishumbha-hantri, Chanda-Munda-vidaravini), her benevolent maternal aspects (Bhavani, Amba, Ambika, Jagadamba), her cosmological roles (Maha-maya, Mula-prakriti, Brahmani), and her epithets from the Devi Mahatmyam. The 108 represents the cosmic completeness — 12 zodiac signs times 9 planets, or other classical symbolic interpretations. (Sources: Stotra Nidhi; Hindupedia Durga; Vedadhara.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the source of the Durga Ashtottara Shatanamavali?
seoFaqs.answer: The Durga Ashtottara Shatanamavali is preserved in classical Shakta tradition associated with the Markandeya Purana and the Durga Saptashati. The 108 names are drawn from the broader Devi Mahatmya corpus and from puranic Devi-naam compilations. Multiple recensions exist — the Vamana Purana version, the Skanda Purana version, the Devi Bhagavata version — and most regional Shakta sampradayas use a version close to the Markandeya tradition. Compilation texts such as Brihat Stotra Ratnakara, the Durga Saptashati paath pothi, and the daily archana manuals used in Devi temples across Bengal, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu and Maharashtra reproduce the 108 names. The hymn is widely circulated through Stotra Nidhi, Vedadhara, Sanskritdocuments.org and Gita Press editions. (Sources: Stotra Nidhi; Hindupedia Durga; Vedadhara.)

seoFaqs.question: How is the Durga Ashtottara Shatanamavali used in puja?
seoFaqs.answer: The Ashtottara Shatanamavali is used principally for archana — the systematic offering of kumkum, akshata (rice grains), red hibiscus flowers, or tulsi leaves at the feet of the deity, one offering per name. The pujari or devotee recites each of the 108 names with reverence, placing one flower or pinch of kumkum at each name. This takes about 12-15 minutes for a careful recitation. The archana is typically embedded within the larger shodashopachara or panchopachara puja sequence — invocation, asana, padya, arghya, achamana, snana, vastra, gandha, pushpa, dhupa, deepa, naivedya, namaskara — with the namavali archana forming the heart of the pushpa-anjali offering. (Sources: Hindupedia Devi puja; Drikpanchang; Stotra Nidhi.)

seoFaqs.question: When is the best time to recite the Durga Ashtottara Shatanamavali?
seoFaqs.answer: Tuesday and Friday are the principal weekdays for any Durga stotra. The supreme annual occasions are the two Navaratris — Sharad Navaratri in Ashvin (especially Durga Ashtami and Mahanavami) and Chaitra Navaratri in spring. Devotees performing daily Devi puja during Navaratri recite the Ashtottara as the primary archana. Other peak occasions include Vijayadashami, Lalita Panchami, Skanda Shashthi, and the regional Devi festivals — Durga Puja in Bengal, Navaratri in Gujarat, Bommai Golu in Tamil Nadu, Dussehra at Mysore. For daily personal devotion the Ashtottara can be recited any morning after the snana and sandhya, taking only 12-15 minutes — making it ideal for householders. (Sources: Drikpanchang Navaratri; Hindupedia; Stotra Nidhi.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Durga Ashtottara phalashruti promise?
seoFaqs.answer: Traditional sources promise that one who recites the Durga Ashtottara Shatanamavali daily with bhakti gains the comprehensive protection of the goddess in all 108 of her named aspects — material protection from enemies, disease, planetary affliction, accidents and untimely death; emotional protection from grief, fear and depression; spiritual protection from negative influences and obstacles to sadhana. Specific rewards include prosperity and abundance in the household, harmony in family relationships, fertility and the birth of virtuous children, success in undertakings, removal of debts and litigation, and ultimately moksha through the maternal grace of Devi. Recitation during Navaratri is said to multiply the benefits, with each name's offering counted as a separate punya. (Sources: Stotra Nidhi; Hindupedia; Vedadhara.)

seoFaqs.question: How is the Durga Ashtottara different from the Durga Sahasranama?
seoFaqs.answer: The Ashtottara Shatanamavali contains 108 names of Durga and is the standard archana namavali used during regular puja while offering flowers — each name preceded by Om and followed by Namah. It takes only 12-15 minutes to recite and is suitable for daily householder use. The Durga Sahasranama, preserved in the Skanda Purana, contains 1000 names of the goddess organised into shloka verses without the Om-namah formula, and takes 45-60 minutes to recite as a complete spiritual practice in itself. Devotees often use the Ashtottara for daily kumkum-archana and reserve the full Sahasranama for Tuesdays, Fridays, Navaratri days, and special Devi vrats. The two complement each other in form and function. (Sources: Stotra Nidhi; Hindupedia Durga Sahasranama; Vedadhara.)

seoFaqs.question: Can the Durga Ashtottara be recited by anyone, including children?
seoFaqs.answer: Yes. As a Pauranika namavali preserved in the open Devi Mahatmya tradition, the Durga Ashtottara Shatanamavali is accessible to all devotees regardless of gender, caste or age, and no formal diksha is required. Children across India are commonly taught the Ashtottara as their first major Devi recitation, often during Navaratri evening puja with the family. Women include it in daily morning puja and during Mangala Gauri vrat. The simplicity of the format — Om plus name plus namah — makes it especially easy to memorise compared to longer shloka stotras. The only customary requirements are cleanliness, an east or north-facing seat, and offering kumkum, red flowers (especially hibiscus), and naivedya of sweets before a Devi image. (Sources: Hindupedia; Stotra Nidhi; Drikpanchang.)
